key_points: Older dorms (Rocky, Mathey, Wilson/First) are genuinely rough quality
No AC in old residential colleges; bathroom supplies frequently run out
No summer storage; no elevators in old dorms are real daily annoyances
key_quotes: I pulled Wilson/First for my freshman and sophomore years and I had a similar experience. It can get pretty gross due to the lack of maintenance.
One thing Princeton needs is to modernize some of the older dorms... having to walk down a flight of stairs and a long hallway to shared bathrooms is rough.
The drastic difference in general quality of life across the dorms is a bit absurd.

key_points: Eating clubs dominate social scene but aren't for everyone—many find them boring
Non-partiers find their niche; a significant minority never engages with clubs
Students described as 'slightly preppy, competitive, and at least someone in your family went here'
key_quotes: Personally I find the eating club parties here really boring and mostly go to events hosted by student extracurricular organizations.
42 percent say they have not had their first kiss yet — never thought of Princeton as a party school.
I had heard Princeton was a really hard-drinking, hard-partying school, and that wasn't wrong; but I found my milk-and-cookies people and had a very full social life.

key_points: Eating clubs are the main upperclass dining option—opt out and your social life shifts
2D Co-Op praised as cheaper alternative saving ~$10k/year with democratic control
Freshman dining largely tied to residential colleges with mixed reviews
key_quotes: I really liked 2D Co-Op more myself. You save like $10k/year extra, you have democratic control, and you learn how to cook with only 1 day a week.
Being part of an eating club was very important to my time at Princeton but it's not for everyone.
I didn't get in but I've heard bad things about the dorms and food tho.

key_points: Student death rate flagged by current junior as roughly one per semester—higher than comparable schools
Workload is genuinely brutal; students describe flailing, withdrawing, then re-enrolling
Collaborative culture softens stress, but grade deflation adds constant pressure
key_quotes: I'm currently a junior and I think it's been about one student death a semester since I started. I'm a little surprised by the frequency.
That was me. It was hard. I took a gap year, then matriculated, flailed, withdrew, took an additional year off, and re-enrolled.
Grade deflation is a nightmare.

key_points: Princeton just eliminated tuition for families earning under $250k—genuinely industry-leading
Assets are counted alongside income; total cost of attendance exceeds tuition alone
Full rides achievable; multiple students report zero expected family contribution
key_quotes: Most people going to Princeton will not have to pay a dime.
Keep in mind that assets, as well as income, will be considered. Tuition ($65k) does not include fees and room and board.
So ironically we have Trump to thank for this then since it seems this is a result of the university wanting to avoid paying the increased endowment tax.

key_points: Honor code just ended—proctored exams now required after 133 years, due to AI cheating
Workload ranked among the most intense of all Ivies; grade deflation is real and documented
Research requirement and senior thesis are distinctive but add serious workload
key_quotes: Princeton is 50x harder than high school. Grade deflation, depression, and stress.
You are about to go from being the smartest person in the room in high school to a place where pretty much everyone was the top of their class.
It's fascinating how horrific the after-effects of the pandemic have been on Princeton's undergraduate program.

key_points: Wintersession cancelled mid-enrollment cycle due to budget constraints—students felt blindsided
Canvas hacked during finals; zero grades posted with exams one week away
Honor code elimination felt abrupt; faculty vs. student communication breakdown evident
key_quotes: I literally got into every Ivy and Stanford, chose Princeton because of wintersession, and they're going to take it away?? Are we deadass.
Dear Hacker, if you want to steal my essays and lab reports, go right ahead. But please grade them. I have several classes with zero grades. Our finals are in a week.
I feel like it's notable that Princeton itself hasn't said anything [about the TCNJ cross-registration]; only TCNJ has.

key_points: Campus is widely praised as stunning—genuinely one of the most beautiful in the US
Princeton town is quiet, expensive, and not student-activity-friendly after hours
NYC and Philly accessible by train but Princeton Junction has massive Amtrak service gaps
key_quotes: I grew up in Princeton and we so rarely saw students off campus that we joked the school was haunted and the students were just ghosts.
Princeton is adorable with lots of cute shops and restaurants but it's definitely not known for its college student friendly activities.
No airport shuttle service for students. Absolutely brutal to pay 30 dollars and get on 3 different trains every time I need to get to/from Newark airport.

key_points: Alumni network described as tight-knit and unusually loyal, especially at Reunions
Undergraduate research requirement gives grads strong PhD and grad school preparation
Finance and consulting pipelines strong; politics and law networks robust
key_quotes: You will never get camaraderie elsewhere like you will at reunions.
Princeton's research reqs provide such a solid foundation for grad school, so that may be a deciding factor.
Princeton graduates are by far the superior academics. Harvard shocked me. It's not worth the money.

key_points: For aided students, Princeton may be the best financial deal in American higher education
Full-pay families face $85k+ annual cost of attendance—still hard to justify without aid
One commenter chose Vanderbilt full scholarship over Princeton full-pay; a real dilemma
key_quotes: We are in a big dilemma in choosing between Princeton (possible full pay) vs Vanderbilt (Cornelius Vanderbilt full tuition scholarship).
Wow amazing, this means now that most people going to Princeton will not have to pay a dime.
So proud to be a Princeton alum — always leading the nation with the best financial aid.

key_points: Intensely academic, collaborative not cutthroat—students suffer together rather than against each other
Preppy, legacy-heavy reputation has basis in reality but doesn't define everyone
Small campus means tight community; isolation from 'real world' is a genuine tradeoff
key_quotes: Going to Princeton is like going to Professor Xavier's mutant school. People on the surface will be normal 18 year olds.
It was extremely hard but extremely collaborative. Seriously I felt that everyone was so kind and helpful, even when it did not benefit them whatsoever.
My friends who go to less 'prestigious' schools are just as happy as I am, and there are people who hate it here because they only went for the name.

key_points: Mental Health: ~one student death per semester reported by current junior; rate appears elevated
Dorms: older residential colleges have chronic maintenance failures, no AC, and hygiene issues
Honor Code: 133-year-old system ended due to AI cheating epidemic—academic integrity in flux
Administration: Wintersession cancelled after students enrolled specifically for that program
key_quotes: It's been about one student death a semester since I started. For alumni who were here in past years, is this typical?
Used pads in the laundry room... piss on the floor... I have seen things beyond comprehension. We would have less mice if you cleaned up after yourselves.
What I'm sure of is that increasing concerns over academic integrity violations, including the use of AI, drove the end of unproctored exams.

key_points: IAS and public lectures give access to world-class researchers—open to everyone in town
Free Adobe Creative Suite, NYT subscription, and thousands of ebooks with student NetID
Novogratz Bridge Year lets admits defer a year for a funded service experience abroad
key_quotes: In my experience the vast majority of speaking events and panels are open to the general public.
Using your netID you get free access to the Adobe suite of apps including Photoshop and Premiere Pro.
I'm currently on the Novogratz Bridge Year in Senegal — if you're thinking of applying, there is stuff you should know.
